site.name: Amelie Brazelton
colophon: Set in Newsreader, IBM Plex & Caveat · Minneapolis

hero.headline: Reporting on the people, places & ecologies that shape our living world.
hero.lede: Amelie Brazelton is an environmental journalist and communications professional based in Minneapolis, with reporting and editorial roots from Florida's Gulf Coast to Brazil's Atlantic Forest.

portrait.caption: Museum of Fine Arts, St. Petersburg · 2025

field.title: The tideline.
field.sub: A drifting archive of frames from assignment and travel — Gulf Coast, Brazil, Spain, and the long road between newsrooms.

creative.title: The notebook
creative.subtitle: — essays, in a quieter voice.
creative.intro: Where the reporting ends and the reflecting begins. Personal essays and literary nonfiction, entered through the image that started each one. Tap a frame to read.

social.title: Design for the feed.
social.sub: Graphics, flyers and campaign assets built across three years of social media work for small arts and environmental organizations.

clips.title: Gulfportians work toward a living shoreline in Gulfport
clips.deck: Student researchers fanned out across the municipal beach with clipboards and watershed maps, asking residents what they value — and what they fear losing.
note: I spent three hours with Priscilla Kidder listening to her story and walking around Veterans Memorial Park.
What was most impactful was listening to her story of the 2024 hurricanes and how that experience influenced her project with the living shoreline.

clips.title: Eckerd College's 16th annual drag show brings the crowd to their feet
clips.deck: The crowd was brought to their feet in Fox Hall on Saturday for Eckerd College's 16th annual drag show. Coordinated by Eckerd College Queer Straight Alliance and Palmetto Productions, the evening dazzled the crowd with performances from professionals and a student competition.
note: It was incredible to cover an event that is so representative and essential to the Eckerd College community.
Especially interviewing a drag queen, it was a special night.

clips.title: There's no losers, only wieners at Gulfport's Get Rescued 2026
clips.deck: Barking, yipping, meowing and howling could all be heard in downtown Gulfport Feb. 28 during the 22nd annual Get Rescued street festival. A fundraiser and supplies drive for the rescues in attendance, the donations this year were light, though still being counted by Suzie King who's been organizing this event for 20 years.
note: Seriously one of my favorite events I've covered — Get Rescued was full of energy and love from everyone for their pets.
It was one of those days that made me think, "man, I love Gulfport."

clips.title: Who will champion our future nature documentaries?
clips.deck: "After living nearly a hundred years on this planet, I now understand that the most important place on earth is not on land but the sea," David Attenborough opens the trailer for his new film, "Oceans."
note: Around the time of David Attenborough's 99th birthday, I considered the future of nature documentaries without his gentle voice and British accent.
This piece was inspired by an aching feeling of loss as a generation of great natural icons passes the torch to the next.

clips.title: Olympian Ilona Maher's influence on Eckerd women's rugby
clips.deck: During the 2024 Summer Olympics, the social media world developed a new obsession with U.S.A rugby player Ilona Maher. Her following skyrocketed at the beginning of July with the Olympic games, and she continued to promote her positive body image message while sharing her experience at the games on Instagram.
note: After a sunset practice, I sat down with the Eckerd Sirens to talk about Ilona Maher, body image, and playing a traditionally masculine sport.

With a circle of girls around me, it was totally new to me to interview so many people at once, especially about sports. Listening to them talk about their rugby family and representation made me realize how culture is connected to everything.

clips.title: Meet Raya: Gulfport Police Department's first therapy dog
clips.deck: Detective Jennifer Crowson introduced Raya Olivia Benson Crowson to the Gulfport Police Department as their first therapy dog last spring, after completing a year-long course in training. The duo went through therapy training at Paws and Stripes College with the Brevard County Sheriff's Office, graduating on April 10, 2025.
note: This was my first non-event article for the Gabber, and I felt so intimidated to be covering the police department. However, those nerves quickly disappeared when I met Raya, who licked my face all over. It's hard to be too serious when your face is covered in dog spit.
I love writing stories about dogs because of the people who care so much about them, like Detective Crowson. And, it means I get to pet a lot of dogs.

documents.title: Coastal Resilience: The Future of Cities with Rising Sea Levels
documents.abstract: As hurricanes grow stronger and seas rise, why do people keep moving to the coast? Between 2021 and 2022 Florida gained 400,000 residents to become the fastest-growing state in 65 years — this paper examines what it will take for coastal cities to adapt rather than simply rebuild after each disaster.

documents.title: Florida Wildlife Corridor: How conservation contributes to Florida's resilience
documents.abstract: Despite its reputation as a red state, Florida has invested more in conservation than almost any other. This paper traces how the 17.7-million-acre Wildlife Corridor sustains the state's resilience — improving species' adaptive capacity, slowing development, and safeguarding the tourism economy.

essays.title: Te amo sempre, filha
essays.excerpt: Returning to Curitiba, and the host mother whose love outran her politics.

essays.title: Red Tide Summer
essays.excerpt: A summer guiding kayak tours on Anna Maria Island, and the red tide that turned the bay to a graveyard.

essays.title: Hello, again.
essays.excerpt: A campus bar, a new girlfriend's friends, and a memory of the first girl I ever practiced kissing.

essays.title: Nine innings, no notebook
essays.excerpt: An afternoon at the ballpark with nothing to file and everything to notice.

essays.title: Becoming the witch for one night
essays.excerpt: Joining the procession — and what it feels like to be looked at instead of looking.

essays.title: Why I keep returning to the MFA
essays.excerpt: On the museum I treat as a second desk, and the paintings that keep me honest.

fieldPhoto.caption: On assignment, interviewing at the Get Rescued races

paragraphs: I write about the seams where people meet the natural world — fisheries, watersheds, harvests, hurricanes — and the cultures that translate between them. My training is in environmental journalism, but my notebook spends as much time in a Brazilian favela or a Seville street market as it does in a Minneapolis newsroom.

I am a Fulbright U.S. Semi-Finalist for the Brazil English Teaching Assistantship, a former Rotary Youth Exchange student to Brazil, and a graduate of Eckerd College's environmental journalism program. I work bilingually in Portuguese and Spanish.

For three years I have led social media strategy for a portfolio of small organizations — work that has taught me to translate the same story across audiences and formats without losing what makes it true.
